symbols: lily or flowers held between the figures — purity and the virginal conception
angel in white — heavenly messenger bearing divine tidings
Mary in red — humanity, the blood of life, and future suffering
church domes in the background — the Church that will be built upon this moment of consent
description: An expressionist painting showing two standing figures facing each other. On the left, a figure in flowing white robes with visible wings extends one arm, holding or offering flowers. On the right, a woman in a red dress with a dark veil or shawl places her hand to her face in a gesture of surprise or contemplation. Between them, a bouquet of yellow and blue flowers is visible. Behind the figures, rounded church domes and architectural forms rise against a warm-toned sky. The brushwork is bold and gestural with vivid colors.
theological_description: The Annunciation is the pivotal moment in salvation history when the angel Gabriel appeared to Mary in Nazareth and declared: 'Greetings, you who are highly favored! The Lord is with you... You will conceive and give birth to a son, and you are to call him Jesus. He will be great and will be called the Son of the Most High' (Luke 1:28-32). Mary's response — 'I am the Lord's servant. May your word to me be fulfilled' (Luke 1:38) — is the fiat that opens the door for the Incarnation. This moment fulfills Isaiah's ancient prophecy: 'The virgin will conceive and give birth to a son, and will call him Immanuel' (Isaiah 7:14). The encounter between Gabriel and Mary represents the intersection of heaven and earth, divine initiative and human cooperation. In Mary's free consent, the entire trajectory of redemption pivots — the Word that was with God from the beginning (John 1:1) prepares to take on flesh. The flowers traditionally symbolize Mary's purity, while the church domes behind them point forward to the community of believers that will arise from this singular act of divine-human collaboration.

scripture_refs.reason: The Annunciation narrative — Gabriel's message to Mary and her consent — is the direct scene depicted
The prophetic announcement that a virgin shall conceive and bear a son called Immanuel
The Word becoming flesh — the Incarnation that the Annunciation initiates
